Now, in essence, here's what all of this chunks up to. As I said earlier, the straight line more than anything, it's the perfect syntax for creating certainty in your prospect, in your client. And syntax, again, is that fancy word for order. So essentially, the straight line represents the order in which you create certainty during a sale. It shows you what to do first, what to do second, what to do third, what to do fourth. And when to corral each of those three tenses together, when to lower the action threshold, when to add on some pain if necessary. Essentially it serves as your blueprint for every single sale, regardless of the prospect's action threshold, regardless of their pain threshold, their initial level of certainty, any of the three tenses. It doesn't matter. It shows you at exactly what point in linguistic encounter you're supposed to line up each of the five elements. In other words, you're not going to start off trying to lower someone's action threshold, and you're not going to start off telling them how great you are as an influencer, right, which is the second tend, until you first had them certain about the greatness of your product, which is the first tend. And you're not going to wait until the very end to raise their pain threshold, understand? You would do that in the beginning as well, so they can sit in your offer in the right way with the right mindset. Again, the straight line is your A, B, C. It's what you're going to be doing first, second, third, and fourth. Now let me just switch hats for a second here and give you a little bit of a different strategy, a way of viewing the straight line, something I call the scales. Okay, now let's pretend for a second that you're an insurance salesman, insurance broker, and you're trying to sell your prospect a new insurance product. Now in the beginning of the sale, on one side of the scale, before you open up your mouth and try to create even the slightest bit of certainty in your prospect, they come into the sale having logical reasons why they should buy now, which is usually why they're there in the first place, looking to find out more about your product. And likewise, they also come into the encounter having emotional reasons why they should buy now. Logical reasons to buy now, emotional reasons to buy now. Then on the other side of the scale, they have logical reasons not to buy now, and emotional reasons not to buy now. Essentially, these are all your negatives and these are all your positives. Make sense? Okay, great. Now very often when an untrained salesman is trying to close someone, one of the huge mistakes they make is that they tend to create lots of logical reasons to buy and also a lot of emotional reasons to buy, but there's no reason to do it now, NOW. You see, it's not enough to simply have reasons to buy, it's got to be to buy now because in the absence of some pain, the average person will simply wait to make a decision. Remember the equation energy in benefits out. Now one way we do that is by answering objections, which are basically reasons not to buy now, both logical and emotional, but that's not exactly the best way to do it in fact. The more elegant way to answer objections is to handle them before they ever come up. You answer objections before they come up. And by the way, since we know there are only so many objections for any one thing, we could embed them in our language patterns from the very beginning and keep doing it as we make our presentation. See, what we're actually doing here just so you know is you move someone down the straight line, you're piling on the positives, right? You're piling on the positives, you're piling on the positives, you're piling on the positives, both logical and emotional, to buy now, to buy now, to buy now and at the same time, you're knocking out the negatives and knocking out the negatives and knocking out the negatives, that's what you're doing. So I want you to visualize this, visualize it as a scale like this. See, on one of those old-fashioned type of scales, the fulcrum right here in the center, in the middle, right, in the beginning, it's balanced. With all the negatives on one side, the reason is not to buy now, not to do it. And here on the positives, all the positive reasons why you should do it now, why you should buy something, right? And they're balanced. So will the person buy? The answer is actually yes. They might buy if they have a very, very low action threshold. But typically, they won't buy because there's got to be at least maybe one more extra positive and that makes it enough and then bam, some with a low action threshold say screw it, I'll just buy. And that's me, right? But what happens is that people with higher action thresholds, there has to be a more dramatic difference between the positives and the negatives. Essentially, they have to be absolutely certain, no seeds of doubt left. So it's like the scale with these sort of overwhelming positives on one side of the scale, right? And there's almost really no negative less than the other side of the scale and boom, when you move them down the straight line and you're running those loops which you're going to be doing in another module, you'll start knocking out negatives, knocking out negatives, adding on positives, a lower the action threshold and bam, that's when people start to buy. So in other words, what you would not do is you would not call someone up right away and say, hey buddy, let's see, I got this great product, let's do it. They'll say, well no, let me think about it. They'll say send me some information. You don't just ask for the order right away. You start off by building up some of your positives and knocking out some negatives. And as you do that, you start making progress. The scale slowly starts to tip. Are they saying yes? No, not yet, unless they have a very low action threshold. So let's take somebody who has a reasonably high action threshold. So you're not seeing progress yet in terms of them saying yes, I'll do it, but you're still making massive progress. So as you take someone down the straight line, you're going from here to here. So you're saying to yourself, damn, they're not buying, they're not buying, but you still know that you're making progress. It's just below the surface. And we can tell them we start to make progress in a couple of different ways. And what is the change in tonality? See after I give them my opening presentation, I'll always ask for the order just to gauge their level of certainty. And when they want to think about it, I'll say, well, let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? And they'll typically say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. Now you want to be at a 10, which sounds like, yeah, it's great, great, I love the idea. So I know I have some work to do here. I have to actually raise their level of certainty about my idea, about the product itself. I have to move them to a higher state of certainty. So let's say I give them another language pattern, a very powerful bullet as to why my product truly is the best thing since sliced bread. And I get them excited. And at the end, when I ask them that question, and you see what I'm saying, do you like the idea? And they say, oh, yeah, no, Jordan, I love the idea. It's great. Where are they now? Now they're like at a 9 or a 10. They're very high on the certainty scale. But do they buy now? Probably not, but I made a lot of progress. I've started to tip the scales in my favor. I made progress before they actually say yes. The biggest mistake that rookie salespeople make is they do not understand the word no or let me think about it. Oh, he said he wants to think about it? Forget it, the sales over. When a prospect says no or let me think about it, let me call you back, that's when the sale begins. Any monkey can read through a sales script. It's what happens after the prospect says, let me think about it. That's when true influence begins. And you'll know you're making progress and you're probably starting to get more certain about things when they start to give you what we call BS. And that's not bullshit. It's buy signals. You run through a very powerful loop and ask for the order, and they'll say, how much does it cost? Which is very different than let me think about it. Ah, that's a buy signal. Or they'll say, what was the thing you said about that feature and what benefit would that give me? Boom, another buy signal. What that represents here on the scales is that you've knocked out some negatives now, you've built up some more positives, and the scale is starting to move. Did they buy yet? Not necessarily. So you keep doing it and you keep running your loops and then you ask for the order of the third time. They're the fourth time. And then eventually you say, with the utmost sincerity at the right time, just give me one shot and believe me, you will not be sorry. Sound fair enough? And bam. Just like that out of nowhere, they're like, all right, sure, I'll do it. That's how it happens. They say, no, no, no, no, no. And all of a sudden, bam, they say yes. And it might have been that one little statement of pennyweight at the end of the scale, that little tiny thing, that last negative you knocked off that caused them to make that final shift. It could have been a one more positive you piled on, but most of the work, in fact, almost all of the work was done before they finally said yes. It was just done below the waterline, below the surface. But before we actually close, I want to just go back to this idea of pain for one more second and how your pain threshold and your action threshold interrelate. Essentially, what happens is, please put the picture up of the scales, the fulcrum. Okay, now notice here, the fulcrum in a typical scale is where, right in the middle, that's where your fulcrum is, what happens essentially is someone that has a very high action threshold, the fulcrum will not be in the middle. The fulcrum is very, very far over to the side of positives. In other words, the positives get very little weight to them. When you put a positive on, nothing really happens because the bulk of the scale is on the other side of the fulcrum. So someone with a high action threshold, each positive doesn't really matter that much, and even one tiny negative has massive consequences. That's what a high action threshold is. So someone with a low action threshold, their fulcrum is on one side of the scale, someone with a very high action threshold, it's on the other side of the scale. And essentially what you do with the pain threshold is when you add it on, you're essentially moving the fulcrum back into the middle again, and when you do that, what happens? The force is massively increased on what you already did, and boom, the sale closes. And that essentially is the straight line logically in a nutshell. Now the next module we do, which is real tactic is looping, you're going to see the straight line at a whole different level of intensity, of certainty in me, I'll be really kind of getting into this sort of hardcore aspects of it and showing you truly how to use this stuff to close anyone who's closable. But for right now, just focus on just grasping the fundamentals one by one separately at first, and then slowly but surely as we go through all the other modules here, we're going to put the pieces together, and I promise you one thing, if you practice this even this much, you will quickly get to the point where you'll be able to close anyone who's closable. And that is truly what the straight line is all about.
